---
title: "Replication of Dean Karlan and John A. List (2007)"
subtitle: "Does Price Mater in Charitable Giving? Evidence from a Large-Scale Natural Field Experiment"
author: Damilola Sule
date: April 21, 2026
jupyter: python3
---


## Introduction

Karlan and List (2007) study whether matching grants increase charitable giving. In their experiment previous donors were randomly assigned to receive different fundraising letters. Some people were offered a matching grant while others were not. This allows the authors to test whether financial incentives affect whether people donate and how much they give in a real world setting. This is an important question because nonprofits use matching campaigns but it has never been clear how effective they actually are.
This blog post, I replicate some of the main results from the paper. First, I check whether the treatment and control groups are similar by reproducing parts of Table 1. Then, I replicate the main treatment effect from Table 2A Panel A which looks at how matching offers affect the probability of giving. I also estimate one of the regression results from Table 3. And lastly I include a graph to better visualize the difference between the treatment and control groups.

## Main Analysis

Table 2A Panel A in Karlan and List (2007) shows the main effect of the matching grant on whether people donate. The outcome I focus on is whether an individual made a donation. I compare the average donation rate between the treatment group and the control group.

 ```{python}
 #| echo: false
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_stata("AERtables1-5.dta")

treat_mean = df[df["treatment"] == 1]["gave"].mean()
control_mean = df[df["treatment"] == 0]["gave"].mean()

diff = treat_mean - control_mean

treat_mean, control_mean, diff
```
```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd

results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["Control", "Treatment", "Difference"],
    "Mean Donation Rate": [control_mean, treat_mean, diff]
})

results
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
print(round(diff, 4))
```

The results show that individuals who received a matching grant offer were more likely to donate than those in the control group. The difference is about 0.00042.

This closely matches the result reported in the paper, confirming that I successfully replicated the main finding.

## Result of Table 2A
To add to the replication, I present the main result from Table 2A graphically.

The bar chart below shows the average donation rate for individuals who received a matching grant offer compared to those who did not. The treatment group clearly has a higher donation rate. This result can also be interpreted as a causal effect. Since the treatment was randomly assigned, the treatment and control groups should be similar on average before the experiment. This means that the difference in donation rates is caused by the matching grant offer rather than other factors.


```{python}
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

groups = ["Control", "Treatment"]
means = [control_mean, treat_mean]

plt.figure()
plt.bar(groups, means)
plt.title("Donation Rates by Treatment Status")
plt.ylabel("Mean Donation Rate")
plt.xlabel("Group")
plt.show()
```
